allopack.title: Central Idea and Evidence — Reading Like a Detective
allopack.author: AlloFlow community catalog (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.7.2 (determine two or more central ideas in a text and analyze their development over the course of the text); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.7.1 (cite several pieces of textual evidence to support analysis of what the text says explicitly as well as inferences); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.7.8 (trace and evaluate the argument and specific claims in a text)

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

In elementary school you found THE main idea. In middle school, texts get harder: they carry more than one central idea, and they build those ideas across paragraphs. You are moving from finding to tracing.

1. Read **More Than One Thread** — it upgrades everything you already knew.
2. Learn the terms in the **Glossary**, then win the **Crossword**.
3. Keep the **Evidence Moves** chart open while you work.
4. Sort the statements in **Claim, Evidence, or Neither?**
5. Take the quiz — it uses real passages and asks you to CITE, not just pick.
6. Build an argument with the **Sentence Frames**.
7. Read the FAQ if you are curious how this works on things designed to persuade you.

The upgrade this year: never say 'the text says' without pointing to WHERE. Evidence has an address. 🔎

objectives.label: I backed up a claim about something I read by quoting exact words from it

history.title: More Than One Thread
history.data:
## More Than One Thread

In earlier grades you learned to find the main idea. That skill still works. But middle-school texts raise the difficulty in two ways, and this year you meet both.

First, a longer text usually has more than one **central idea** running through it at once. An article about a river cleanup might carry one central idea about pollution science and another about a community organizing itself. Neither is wrong. A good reader tracks both threads.

Second, ideas **develop** across a text. A writer might introduce a claim in paragraph one, complicate it in the middle, and qualify it at the end. Where a paragraph has one main idea, a whole article has ideas that move and change. Your job is to trace how, not just to name what.

Now the part that separates strong readers from guessers: **evidence has an address.**

A **claim** is a statement someone wants you to accept. "Bike lanes make streets safer." **Evidence** is the specific proof offered for it: a statistic, a study, an example, a quotation. A claim without evidence is just an opinion wearing a confident voice.

When you answer a question about a text, you are making a small claim of your own, and it needs the same thing you would demand of anyone else. Not "the text says pollution went down." Instead: "In paragraph four, the author states that fish returned within two years, which shows the cleanup worked." You named where, you quoted what, you explained why it matters. That is **citing evidence**, and it is the core skill of every subject you will ever study, not just English.

Be careful of a common trap. Evidence must actually support the claim, not just sit near it. A detail can be true, on-topic, and still not prove the point. If the claim is "the cleanup worked" and the evidence is "the river is 30 miles long," that fact is true and about the river, but it proves nothing about the cleanup. Relevance is not the same as relatedness.

One more move for this year: writers of persuasion mix claims with **loaded language** and leave out inconvenient facts. Reading like a detective means asking not only "what is the central idea?" but "what evidence is offered, is it relevant, and what has been left out?" That question protects you far beyond any test.

So the upgrade is small to say and large to do: find the threads, watch them develop, and never accept a claim without checking its evidence — including your own.

data.def: A main point that runs through a text — longer texts often have more than one.
A statement someone wants you to accept as true.
Specific proof offered for a claim: a fact, example, statistic, or quotation.
To point to the exact place in a text that supports your answer.
Actually proving the claim, not just related to the topic.
How an idea grows or changes across a text.
A conclusion you build from clues the text does not state outright.
A claim backed by reasons and evidence.
Based on facts, not personal feelings.
A leaning toward one side that can shape what a writer includes or leaves out.
The exact words from a text, shown inside quotation marks.
To back up a claim with evidence and reasoning.

sections.label: NAME THE CLAIM
bullets: A claim is what someone wants you to accept
State it in one clear sentence
Your answers are small claims too
sections.iconPrompt: a speech bubble with a bold sentence

sections.label: POINT TO IT
bullets: Never say 'the text says' without pointing
sections.iconPrompt: a finger pointing at a highlighted line

sections.label: EXPLAIN WHY
bullets: Connect the evidence to the claim
Evidence without explanation is half an answer
sections.iconPrompt: an arrow linking a quote to a claim

sections.label: CHECK RELEVANCE
bullets: True and on-topic is NOT enough
Ask: does this actually prove the claim?
Also ask: what has been left out?
sections.iconPrompt: a checkmark and a question mark side by side

categories.label: Neither — just a fact off the point

items.content: Our town should build more bike lanes
A study found bike-lane streets had 40% fewer injuries
Recycling programs are worth the cost
The city recovered $2 for every $1 spent on recycling last year
The recycling trucks are painted green
Later school start times improve teen health
In the trial schools, average sleep rose by 34 minutes a night
The school building has three floors
Doctors surveyed reported fewer sick days after the change

history.title: Cite It or Lose It

questions.question: How is a 7th-grade central-idea task different from a younger main-idea task?
options: A text can have more than one central idea, and ideas develop across the text
There is no difference — it is the same skill renamed
You no longer need to use evidence
Central ideas are always stated in the first sentence
questions.correctAnswer: A text can have more than one central idea, and ideas develop across the text

questions.question: What is the difference between a claim and evidence?
options: A claim is always false and evidence is always true
A claim is what someone wants you to accept; evidence is the specific proof for it
A claim is longer than evidence
They are two words for the same thing
questions.correctAnswer: A claim is what someone wants you to accept; evidence is the specific proof for it

questions.question: Passage: 'The garden club planted 200 trees downtown. Within five years, summer street temperatures there dropped by three degrees.' Which sentence is the best EVIDENCE that the trees cooled the streets?
options: 'The garden club planted 200 trees downtown'
Neither sentence is evidence
'Within five years, summer street temperatures there dropped by three degrees'
Both sentences are claims
questions.correctAnswer: 'Within five years, summer street temperatures there dropped by three degrees'

questions.question: A claim states 'the new library hours help working families.' Which fact is NOT relevant evidence, even though it is true?
options: Evening visits by parents rose 60% after the hours changed
A survey shows most weekday visitors now come after 5 p.m.
Checkouts of children's books increased on weeknights
The library building was repainted last spring
questions.correctAnswer: The library building was repainted last spring

questions.question: Which answer CITES evidence properly?
options: The text basically says people liked it
In paragraph two, the author writes that 'attendance doubled,' which shows the program was popular
I just feel like the program worked
Everyone knows programs like this are popular
questions.correctAnswer: In paragraph two, the author writes that 'attendance doubled,' which shows the program was popular

questions.question: Passage: 'Scientists tagged 50 sea turtles. Every one returned to the exact beach where it hatched to lay its own eggs, some traveling over 1,000 miles.' State one central idea and cite the specific evidence for it.
questions.expectedAnswer: Central idea: sea turtles return to their birth beach to nest. Evidence: 'Every one returned to the exact beach where it hatched to lay its own eggs, some traveling over 1,000 miles.' The all-50 result and the long distances show the behavior is consistent and deliberate.

questions.question: The reading says 'relevance is not the same as relatedness.' Explain the difference and give your own example of a true but irrelevant detail.
questions.expectedAnswer: A detail can be about the same topic yet not prove the claim. For a claim that 'the new bus route saves students time,' the detail 'the buses are yellow' is related to buses but does not support the claim about time. Relevant evidence would be actual travel-time data.

reflections.text: Think of an advertisement you have seen. What was its claim, and what evidence (if any) did it actually offer?
When is it hardest for you to find evidence in a text: long passages, or ideas that are only implied? Why?

history.meta: 5 MCQ + 2 short answer • real passages, citing required

history.title: Build an Argument

items.text: One central idea of this text is ____.
The author develops this idea by first ____, then ____.
In paragraph ____, the text states '____,' which shows ____.
A second piece of evidence is '____,' and it supports the claim because ____.
The detail about ____ is true but not relevant, because ____.
What the author leaves out is ____, which matters because ____.

data.rubric:
| Criteria | 1 — Starting | 3 — Getting There | 5 — Analyst |
|---|---|---|---|
| Central idea | Gives a topic word | A central-idea sentence | Identifies a central idea AND how it develops |
| Evidence | No citation | Quotes once, no location | Cites 2+ times with location and exact words |
| Reasoning | Restates the quote | Links evidence to claim once | Explains each link AND tests relevance |

history.meta: 6 frames • rubric • two citations required at level 5

data.question: Can a text really have two main ideas without contradicting itself? 🤔
data.answer: Yes — they are usually about different aspects of the same topic. An article on a hurricane might carry one central idea about the storm's science and another about how a community recovered. Both are true and both matter.

data.question: What is the difference between evidence and just an opinion?
data.answer: Evidence can be checked by someone else: a statistic, a study, a quotation, an observation. An opinion is a preference. 'This is the best song' is opinion; 'this song spent ten weeks at number one' is evidence.

data.question: Why do teachers care so much about citing WHERE? 📍
data.answer: Because it is the difference between reading and remembering. Pointing to the exact spot proves you got it from the text, and it lets someone check your reasoning. Every field — science, law, history — runs on this.

data.question: How does this help me with ads and social media?
data.answer: Enormously. Persuasion mixes real claims with loaded language and missing facts. Asking 'what is the evidence, is it relevant, and what got left out?' is a defense you will use your whole life.

data.question: What if the central idea is never actually stated?
data.answer: Then you build it by inference — a conclusion from the clues. That is harder and completely legitimate; you just have to be able to point to the clues you used.

data.question: Is it cheating to quote the text instead of using my own words? ✍️
data.answer: The opposite — quoting the exact words IS the skill. You quote the evidence, then add your own words to explain why it supports your point. Both parts are required.
